subject: [PATCH v6 2/2] mm: use mapping_max_folio_order() for force_thp_readahead order

reviews.author: Pedro Falcato
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Pedro Falcato noted that memory pressure is still a problem even on 2MB folios and suggested it's not a significant issue in this case because the system is requesting THPs and getting them, albeit inefficiently.

Reviewer Pedro Falcato noted that the condition MAX_PAGECACHE_ORDER <= HPAGE_PMD_ORDER is always true, and suggested removing the min3() function in the diff as it is redundant.

Reviewer Pedro Falcato suggested limiting the THP order to 32MB instead of 2M, arguing that it simplifies logic while still clamping maximum folio order for architectures not supporting 32MiB folios.

Reviewer Pedro Falcato disagreed with the argument that raising the THP cap to 2M would not cause problems, pointing out that increasing the size of folios can lead to thrashing and TLB thrashing, citing past reports of such issues. He noted that changing the logic for deciding what should be a THP will always affect someone.
reviews.raw_body:
For what it's worth, memory pressure is still a problem even on 2MB folios. But
yes, I understand.

For what it's worth, I don't think it's such a big issue in this case - you're
asking for THPs, and you're getting them - though maybe not efficiently because
these aren't at the PMD level. But this is also true for e.g x86 where an
an order-3 folio or order-7 folio are completely useless for the hardware, since
there is no mTHP to speak of (and, with that, you also lose PG_active and
PG_reference precision as you only keep one of each for each folio, amongst
other tradeoffs).

---

Right, good point. (well, I suppose it will not be true in the future as
someone tries to harness PMD-level cont bits)

---

So maybe limit to 32MB? It's still arbitrary but at least you get simpler
logic. If the architecture does not support 32MiB folios, it will clamp
the maximum folio order to HPAGE_PMD_ORDER, and you get the same result.

Does this sound correct?

---

I don't think this is a valid argument. You can also have reports that now
folios are too large and they're regressing workloads because of thrashing/
compaction/possibly TLB thrashing. And indeed we have several reports of that
(https://lore.kernel.org/linux-fsdevel/20260403193535.9970-1-dipiets@amazon.it/
comes to mind, plus all of the anon THP regressions over the years, including
in $current_year).

Bottom line is that changing things will always affect someone :) Particularly
since the logic we have is not too careful at deciding what should or should
not be a THP (both in anon and file cases). And if (once?) we make it smarter,
it will surely also regress someone!

-- 
Pedro

reviews.author: Usama Arif (author)
reviews.summary:
Author responded to concerns about the patch's impact on memory pressure by explaining that the current code is correct in maintaining existing behavior and providing large folios to architectures that currently don't get them, but acknowledged that raising the cap could be difficult.

Author agrees that replacing SZ_2M with SZ_32M in the patch would be correct but expresses concern about the size being too large.

Author acknowledged that large file folios can cause memory pressure and agreed that their patch provides a controlled way to provide large folios to architectures that currently don't get them, but did not address the specific concern about the 16K ARM case.

Author agrees to keep the 2M cap initially while addressing issues with it, but asks for feedback from other reviewers.
reviews.raw_body:
So there is hardware TLB coalescing on almost every new AMD CPU, I believe it is
at 32kB, and we would benefit from that. On top of that, we would reduce pagefaults.

---

Yes, so if we replace it with SZ_32M, it sounds correct. I just think
the 32M size is too large. But as you pointed out, even 2M can be too large...

---

Ack, and yes we have seen both thrashing and compaction issues due to xfs
large folios in the fleet with x86..

---

Yes completely agree on this as well.

So personally I do have a preference of keeping the cap at 2M atleast initially
while we currently try and solve the issues we see with 2M alone. As we are already
seeing reports of thrashing and compaction with just 2M, I dont think the logic
in this patch with just an if else is that complicated.

Matthew, Jan, do you have any thoughts or strong preferences on cap size?

Thanks!

2026-05-29.patch_summary: This patch modifies the force_thp_readahead order calculation to use mapping_max_folio_order() instead of a hardcoded value. The previous code had issues with memory pressure due to large file folios, and this change aims to provide a controlled way to allocate larger folios on architectures that currently don't support them. The new approach sets the thp_order to the minimum of the maximum folio order for the mapping and 2M, ensuring that the allocation is not too aggressive and causing excessive memory pressure. This patch also removes an unnecessary min3() call in the original code.
